content:
6 min read
Just now

--

The most spectacular thing an AI tool can make is a wide shot. The most important thing in cinema has always been a face. A founder’s view.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A face emerging from the dark. The oldest shot in cinema, and the one AI is least sure of.In 1928 Carl Theodor Dreyer made a film almost entirely out of one woman’s face. The Passion of Joan of Arc has barely an establishing shot in it. Dreyer built a full medieval set and then refused to show you most of it. He pushed the camera into Renée Falconetti’s face, stripped away her makeup, and let the close-up carry the work that sets, costumes and spectacle usually do.
Pauline Kael called it the finest performance ever recorded on film. Roger Ebert said you cannot know the history of silent film unless you know that face. Falconetti made one movie in her life. One. And almost a hundred years later it is still the thing other performances get measured against.
Now open any AI video tool and watch what it wants to give you. Dragons over a burning city. A spaceship the size of a country. A drone shot falling down a canyon at golden hour. The machine is happiest at the widest, most expensive end of the lens. Ask it for the thing Dreyer built a masterpiece out of, a human face holding a single emotion across a cut, and you can feel it flinch.
That flinch is the real ceiling for AI filmmaking. Not resolution. The face.
The face was always the point
Ingmar Bergman said it more plainly than anyone.
The human face is the great subject of the cinema. Everything is there.
He was not being poetic. He built whole films on it. Persona is two women’s faces, at one point literally fused into one. Bergman understood that an audience reads a face faster and deeper than it reads anything else on a screen. Give people a face and they will tell you what the scene is about before a word is spoken.
The Hungarian critic Béla Balázs gave this a name almost a century ago. He called it microphysiognomy: the tiny, half-conscious movements of a face that the close-up makes visible and that you would miss sitting across from someone in real life. A flicker at the corner of the mouth. The eyes deciding something a half-second before the line. Balázs said the close-up opened a new dimension of the soul, and he meant it almost as a technical fact. The lens catches things about a face that a person cannot.
This is the part AI has not cracked, and it is worth asking why a model trained on millions of clips would struggle exactly here. The answer is that spectacle forgives and a face does not. A wide shot of a city can be slightly wrong in a thousand places and you will never notice. A face that is slightly wrong is a face you do not believe. The eyes drift. The expression holds a beat too long, then resets between shots as if nothing happened. We are built to catch precisely that. Every human being alive is an expert at reading faces, which makes the face the one shot where an AI tool has no margin for error.
Spectacle is cheap. A held look is not.
Sergio Leone came at this from the opposite direction and landed in the same place. He gave you the widest landscapes in cinema, then collapsed them into the most extreme close-ups anyone had ever shot. The final standoff in The Good, the Bad and the Ugly is three men in a circle, and Leone cuts tighter and tighter until there is nothing on screen but eyes. No dialogue. The entire tension of the genre lives in a few centimeters of face. He made you wait minutes for it. AI cuts away in seconds, because it does not understand that the waiting was the point.
Hitchcock turned the same instinct into a rule. He treated the close-up as currency you spend carefully.
Don’t go putting a close-up where you don’t need it, because later on you will need it.
He told Truffaut that the size of the image is used for dramatic purposes only. A close-up means something because most shots are not close-ups. Save it, and when you finally push in, the audience leans forward with you. Spend it on everything and it buys nothing.
That is direction, and it is exactly the layer an AI tool skips. The model will happily render a face. It has no opinion about when to cut to that face, how long to hold it, or whether this is the moment worth spending the close-up on. None of those are rendering problems. They are decisions, and the decisions are still yours.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The model renders the face. Everything else on the right is still the director’s call.The second problem: the face has to stay itself
There is a second thing about faces that wide shots never demand. A face has to be the same face in the next shot.
This is where AI quietly falls apart. A model generates each shot as a fresh event. The actor in shot four is a cousin of the actor in shot three. Close enough to fool a thumbnail, not close enough to carry a scene. In a wide shot nobody notices that an extra in the background changed. In a close-up, the whole film depends on this being the same person who was hurting two shots ago, with the same scar, the same tired eyes, the same particular face.
Continuity used to be a person’s job. The script supervisor sat beside the camera and remembered everything: which hand held the glass, how the hair fell, what the face was doing on the last take so the next one would match. AI has no script supervisor. It has no memory of who this character is or what their face was carrying thirty seconds of screen time ago. So the most demanding shot in cinema, the one Dreyer built an entire film from, is also the shot where AI’s lack of memory shows the most.
What this means if you are building with AI
So if you are making something with AI in the loop, the work is not getting a prettier face out of the generator. The work is deciding when the scene turns to a face, how long it stays there, and making sure that face is still itself three shots later. That is the same job Dreyer, Bergman and Leone were doing. The tools changed. The decision did not.
This is the part of the problem I spend my days on. ScreenWeaver is the workspace between writing and production, where a scene’s intent, including who a character is and what their face is meant to carry, stays attached to the shot instead of being rolled fresh every generation. AI hands you options. It will not give you a reason to push in on a face, and it will not remember that face for you. That part is still direction.
Falconetti never made another film. She did not need to. One face, no makeup, no words, and a director who understood that the close-up was the whole instrument. The machines can now generate almost anything you describe to them. They are still strangely afraid of the simplest and oldest shot in cinema: a person’s face holding still while everything happens behind the eyes.
That fear is your opening. Walk through it.
If you are building films with AI and you care about the part the model keeps skipping, that is the work I am trying to make easier at ScreenWeaver.
